description: UE5 debugging tools: UE_LOG, log categories, PrintString, Visual Logger, Gameplay Debugger, stat commands, and console commands

entries.title: UE_LOG — C++ Logging System
entries.description: UE_LOG is the primary logging macro in UE5 C++. It writes formatted messages to the Output Log with severity levels and category filtering. Essential for debugging any C++ code.
entries.whatItIs: UE_LOG is a variadic macro that writes formatted text to UE5's Output Log panel and log files. It takes a log category, verbosity level (Fatal/Error/Warning/Display/Verbose), and printf-style format string. Custom log categories let you filter output by system.
whenToUse: Debugging any C++ code — print variable values, execution flow, and state to the Output Log
Error reporting — log errors and warnings that persist in log files for post-mortem analysis
Tracking game flow — log when key events happen (player spawned, level loaded, save completed)
Performance investigation — log timestamps to find where time is being spent
Shipping builds — UE_LOG persists in log files even in packaged builds (at appropriate verbosity)
whenNotToUse: For player-facing messages — use UMG widgets or HUD text instead
For quick visual debugging on screen — use GEngine->AddOnScreenDebugMessage or PrintString (faster to read in real-time)
When you need structured data logging for analytics — use a proper telemetry system
In extremely hot code paths (inner loops running millions of times) — logging has I/O overhead
commonMistakes: Using UE_LOG(LogTemp, ...) for everything instead of creating custom categories — makes filtering impossible in large projects
Forgetting the * before FString in format: UE_LOG(LogTemp, Log, TEXT("%s"), *MyString) — missing * crashes
Using %s with FName or FText directly — use *MyName.ToString() or *MyText.ToString()
Logging in Tick without a condition, flooding the Output Log with thousands of lines per second
Not understanding verbosity: Log/Display always shows, Verbose/VeryVerbose are hidden by default — use Log for important info, Verbose for noise
Using UE_LOG in constructors — output may not be visible in the editor log at that point
keyPoints: UE_LOG(CategoryName, Verbosity, TEXT("format"), args...): the basic macro
Verbosity levels: Fatal (crashes), Error (red), Warning (yellow), Display (white), Log (white), Verbose (hidden), VeryVerbose (hidden)
LogTemp: built-in catch-all category. Use for quick debugging only.
Custom category: DECLARE_LOG_CATEGORY_EXTERN(LogMyGame, Log, All) in .h, DEFINE_LOG_CATEGORY(LogMyGame) in .cpp
Format specifiers: %s (TCHAR*/FString*), %d (int), %f (float), %i (int), %lld (int64)
GEngine->AddOnScreenDebugMessage(Key, Duration, Color, Message): on-screen text (faster to see than log)
Output Log panel in the editor: Window > Developer Tools > Output Log
Log files: Saved/Logs/ folder. Latest is the .log file, previous are numbered backups.

entries.title: On-Screen Debug & PrintString
entries.description: Quick visual debugging tools that display text on the game viewport. Faster to read than the Output Log during real-time gameplay testing.
entries.whatItIs: On-screen debug messages and PrintString are immediate visual feedback tools that render text directly in the game viewport. They are the fastest way to see variable values during Play-In-Editor without pausing to check the Output Log.
whenToUse: Quick debugging during PIE — see variable values in real-time without checking the log
Visualizing state changes — display current health, state machine state, or AI behavior
Prototyping and testing — verify that code paths are executing correctly
When the Output Log scrolls too fast to read
whenNotToUse: Shipping/release builds — remove or wrap in #if !UE_BUILD_SHIPPING
Player-facing text — use UMG widgets for proper UI
Persistent logs that need to be reviewed later — use UE_LOG which writes to files
Performance-critical code — on-screen messages have rendering overhead
commonMistakes: Leaving PrintString/AddOnScreenDebugMessage in shipping builds — adds overhead and looks unprofessional
Using Key=-1 for AddOnScreenDebugMessage in Tick, creating a new message every frame that fills the screen — use a positive Key to overwrite the same slot
Not realizing that PrintString in Blueprint has both 'Print to Screen' and 'Print to Log' checkboxes — check both for debugging
Using on-screen messages for data that should be in a proper debug UI — if you need persistent stats, build a debug widget
Forgetting the GEngine null check — GEngine can be null in dedicated server or during shutdown
keyPoints: GEngine->AddOnScreenDebugMessage(Key, Duration, Color, Message): C++ on-screen text
Key=-1: adds a new message each call. Key=positive: overwrites the same slot (great for Tick).
UKismetSystemLibrary::PrintString(): Blueprint-accessible, prints to screen and/or log
Blueprint PrintString node: set Text Color, Duration, bPrintToScreen, bPrintToLog
FString::Printf(TEXT("format"), args): create formatted strings for display
Wrap in #if !UE_BUILD_SHIPPING to auto-strip from release builds
DrawDebugString(): draws text at a 3D world location (good for per-actor labels)

entries.title: Draw Debug Helpers — Visual Debugging
entries.description: Draw debug shapes (lines, spheres, boxes, arrows, capsules) in the 3D world to visualize traces, radii, directions, and spatial logic during development.
entries.whatItIs: The DrawDebug* family of functions renders temporary 3D shapes in the game viewport for visualizing spatial gameplay logic. They are indispensable for debugging traces, collision, AI perception radii, spawn points, and any system that involves positions and directions in 3D space.
whenToUse: Visualizing line traces and sweep traces — see exactly where the ray goes and what it hits
Debugging AI perception cones, hearing radii, or patrol paths
Verifying spawn locations, socket positions, or attachment points
Visualizing collision shapes, trigger volumes, or damage radii
Checking direction vectors (forward, aim direction, velocity) with arrows
whenNotToUse: For permanent in-game visual lines — use spline meshes, decals, or procedural meshes
In shipping builds — wrap in #if ENABLE_DRAW_DEBUG or strip entirely
When you need hundreds of persistent debug shapes — the rendering overhead is significant
For UI or HUD information — use UMG widgets or on-screen messages
commonMistakes: Calling DrawDebugLine in Tick with bPersistent=true, creating permanent lines every frame that tank performance
Using Duration=0 and wondering why shapes disappear immediately — set Duration to a positive value or use -1 for persistent
Forgetting to include DrawDebugHelpers.h — the functions won't be found
Drawing debug shapes in multiplayer on the server — they only render locally, not on clients
Not wrapping in ENABLE_DRAW_DEBUG, causing compile errors in shipping builds where debug drawing is stripped
keyPoints: DrawDebugLine(World, Start, End, Color, bPersistent, Duration, DepthPriority, Thickness)
DrawDebugSphere(World, Center, Radius, Segments, Color, bPersistent, Duration)
DrawDebugBox(World, Center, Extent, Color, bPersistent, Duration)
DrawDebugCapsule(World, Center, HalfHeight, Radius, Rotation, Color)
DrawDebugArrow(World, Start, End, ArrowSize, Color): great for showing directions
DrawDebugPoint(World, Location, Size, Color): mark specific points
DrawDebugCoordinateSystem(World, Location, Rotation, Scale): show XYZ axes at a point
All functions take bPersistent (stays until cleared) and Duration (seconds)
#include "DrawDebugHelpers.h" required in your .cpp file

entries.title: Gameplay Debugger & Visual Logger
entries.description: UE5's built-in advanced debugging tools for AI, gameplay, and system visualization. The Gameplay Debugger shows real-time overlays, and the Visual Logger records data for timeline scrubbing.
entries.whatItIs: The Gameplay Debugger is a real-time overlay system activated with the apostrophe (') key during PIE that displays categorized debug information (AI, EQS, Perception, NavMesh). The Visual Logger records structured debug data over time that can be reviewed frame-by-frame in the Visual Logger window.
whenToUse: Debugging AI behavior — see Behavior Tree state, Blackboard values, Perception, and EQS results in real-time
Debugging navigation — visualize NavMesh, path following, and pathfinding queries
When you need to scrub through time to find when something went wrong (Visual Logger)
When you need structured, categorized debug data beyond simple print statements
whenNotToUse: For simple variable inspection — PrintString or UE_LOG is faster to set up
In shipping builds — both tools are editor/development only
For debugging UI layout — use the UMG Widget Reflector instead
When you just need to check a value once — use a breakpoint in your IDE
commonMistakes: Not knowing the apostrophe (') key activates the Gameplay Debugger — many developers don't know this tool exists
Forgetting to select a specific actor to debug — click on an AI actor while the Gameplay Debugger is active to see its details
Not enabling the Gameplay Debugger categories you need — press numpad keys (0-9) to toggle categories
Overwhelming the Visual Logger with too much data logged every frame — log only when state changes
Forgetting to include VisualLogger/VisualLogger.h and using the UE_VLOG macros incorrectly
keyPoints: Gameplay Debugger: press ' (apostrophe) during PIE to toggle. Click on actors to inspect.
Categories: AI (0), BehaviorTree (1), EQS (2), Perception (3), NavMesh (4) — toggle with numpad
Shows: current BT node, Blackboard values, perception sources, NavMesh around actor
Visual Logger: Window > Developer Tools > Visual Logger. Must enable recording first.
UE_VLOG(Owner, CategoryName, Verbosity, Format, ...): log to Visual Logger from C++
UE_VLOG_LOCATION, UE_VLOG_SEGMENT, UE_VLOG_BOX: log 3D shapes to Visual Logger timeline
Visual Logger lets you scrub time backward — see what the AI saw 5 seconds ago
Custom Gameplay Debugger categories: extend UGameplayDebuggerCategory for your own systems

entries.title: Console Commands & Stat Commands
entries.description: UE5's in-game console provides stat commands for real-time performance monitoring and debug commands for toggling engine features. Essential for optimization and debugging.
entries.whatItIs: The in-game console (activated with ~ or ` key) accepts text commands that control engine behavior, display performance statistics, and toggle debug visualizations. Stat commands show real-time overlays for FPS, draw calls, memory, and system-specific metrics.
whenToUse: Performance profiling — see FPS, frame time, draw calls, and memory usage in real-time
Debugging rendering — toggle wireframe, collision, lights, fog, etc.
Debugging networking — see bandwidth, replication, and actor relevancy
Quick testing — change CVars (console variables) to tweak engine settings without restarting
whenNotToUse: For player-facing settings — use a proper settings menu UI
For persistent configuration changes — use DefaultEngine.ini or Project Settings
In shipping builds for cheats — console is disabled by default in shipping
commonMistakes: Not knowing that ~ (tilde/backtick) opens the console — many beginners never discover it
Using stat commands in packaged builds where the console is disabled — enable it explicitly or use in-editor only
Leaving debug commands (god, fly, ghost) enabled in builds that ship to testers
Not using 'stat unit' as the first step in performance investigation — it shows the key bottleneck (Game, Draw, GPU)
Confusing stat fps (simple FPS) with stat unit (detailed frame breakdown) — stat unit is far more useful
keyPoints: Open console: press ~ (tilde) or ` (backtick) during PIE or packaged build (if enabled)
stat fps: show framerate. stat unit: show Game/Draw/GPU frame times (more useful!).
stat game: gameplay thread stats. stat scenerendering: render thread stats.
stat memory: memory usage breakdown. stat streaming: texture/mesh streaming stats.
stat startfile / stat stopfile: record a profiling session to disk for Unreal Insights.
show collision: visualize collision shapes. show navigation: visualize NavMesh.
viewmode wireframe: render in wireframe. viewmode lit: back to normal.
slomo 0.5: slow motion. slomo 1: normal speed.
Custom console commands: UCheatManager subclass or UFUNCTION(Exec) on PlayerController.
CVars: r.ScreenPercentage=50 (render at half res), sg.PostProcessQuality=0 (disable post-process)
